data.title: Is KB Home (KBH) Pricing Look Sustainable After Recent Share Price Strength
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether KB Home's share price still offers value at current levels, it helps to start by looking at how the market has been treating the stock recently.
  • KB Home closed at US$55.74, with returns of 7.3% over the last 7 days, 9.0% over the past 30 days, a 2.3% decline year to date, and 5.7% over the last year. This gives you a mix of shorter term momentum and a more modest recent track record.
  • These moves sit against a backdrop of ongoing attention on U.S. housing affordability, interest rates, and homebuilding activity, which often influences how investors think about companies like KB Home. Broader discussions around supply constraints and demand in key housing markets have also kept the sector in focus and have shaped sentiment toward builders.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, KB Home scores a 3. The rest of this article will walk through what that means across different valuation methods, as well as a further way to think about value that can help tie everything together.

Find out why KB Home's 5.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: KB Home Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model looks at the value of a stock by projecting all future dividend payments and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what those future cash returns to shareholders are worth in present terms.

For KB Home, the model uses a dividend per share of about US$1.01, a return on equity of 15.73% and a payout ratio of 10.92%. Based on these inputs, dividend growth is capped at 3.41%, even though the broader growth input before capping is 14.01%. This cap is intended to keep the long term dividend growth assumption more restrained and potentially more sustainable.

Putting these pieces together, the DDM produces an estimated intrinsic value of US$16.73 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$55.74, the model implies the stock is trading at a much higher price than this dividend-based estimate of intrinsic value, with an intrinsic discount suggesting it is about 233.2% above that estimate.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Dividend Discount Model (DDM) analysis suggests KB Home may be overvalued by 233.2%. Discover 58 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for KB Home.

Approach 2: KB Home Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like KB Home, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to current profits, which is often how investors compare established companies within the same sector.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to be associated with a lower P/E.

KB Home currently trades on a P/E of 9.96x. That sits below the Consumer Durables industry average of 12.51x and also below the broader peer group average of 14.35x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for KB Home is 11.14x, which represents the P/E level that would be expected given factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio framework can be more tailored than a simple comparison to peers or the industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all builders deserve the same multiple. Comparing 9.96x to the Fair Ratio of 11.14x suggests KB Home’s earnings are being priced at a discount.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Why You Might Be Interested In Grupo SBF S.A. (BVMF:SBFG3) For Its Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Readers hoping to buy Grupo SBF S.A. (BVMF:SBFG3) for its dividend will need to make their move shortly, as the stock is about to trade ex-dividend. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Meaning, you will need to purchase Grupo SBF's shares before the 27th of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 31st of December.

The company's next dividend payment will be R$0.5428511 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed R$0.55 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Grupo SBF stock has a trailing yield of around 4.2% on the current share price of R$13.17. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. That's why it's good to see Grupo SBF paying out a modest 39% of its earnings. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 58% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

See our latest analysis for Grupo SBF

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. That's why it's comforting to see Grupo SBF's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 24% per annum for the past five years.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Grupo SBF has delivered an average of 59% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past three years of dividend payments. It's exciting to see that both earnings and dividends per share have grown rapidly over the past few years.

Final Takeaway

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Grupo SBF? From a dividend perspective, we're encouraged to see that earnings per share have been growing, the company is paying out less than half of its earnings, and a bit over half its free cash flow. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.

With that in mind, a critical part of thorough stock research is being aware of any risks that stock currently faces. For example, we've found 1 warning sign for Grupo SBF that we recommend you consider before investing in the business.

data.description:

  • The stock has been fairly steady over the last week and month, with returns of 0.0% over 7 days and 0.5% over 30 days. However, the longer term picture includes a 12.2% return year to date and 72.2% over the past year, alongside an 11.7% decline over three years.

This background helps explain why some shareholders are reassessing what counts as a reasonable price for the stock today.
  • Right now, IHS Holding scores a 5 out of 6 valuation score. The rest of this article will unpack what that means across different valuation methods, before finishing with a broader way to think about what “fair value” really is.

IHS Holding delivered 72.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Telecom industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: IHS Holding Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value. For IHS Holding, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that focuses on cash available to shareholders.

IHS Holding has last twelve month free cash flow of about $584.3 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections put free cash flow in 2030 at around $461.1 million, with a series of annual estimates between 2026 and 2035 that are discounted back to present value. All these forecasts are expressed in US$ and Simply Wall St extrapolates beyond the analyst horizon to complete the 10 year path.

When these projected cash flows are discounted and added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $23.31 per share. Against a current share price of $8.25, this implies a 64.6% discount, which points to IHS Holding trading well below this DCF estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests IHS Holding is undervalued by 64.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for IHS Holding.

Approach 2: IHS Holding Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you are paying per share to the earnings that each share generates. It gives a quick sense of how many years of current earnings the market is willing to pay for.

What counts as a “normal” P/E will vary. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk can pull that multiple down. Context therefore matters when you look at any single P/E number.

IHS Holding currently trades on a P/E of about 4.46x. This is below the Telecom industry average P/E of about 17.23x and below the peer average of around 8.22x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for IHS Holding is 12.28x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

Because the Fair Ratio blends these fundamentals rather than relying only on simple peer or industry comparisons, it can offer a more tailored anchor for your expectations. With a current P/E of 4.46x versus a Fair Ratio of 12.28x, IHS Holding screens as trading below this Fair Ratio based view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

🐂 IHS Holding Bull Case

Fair value: US$9.00 per share

Revenue growth assumption: 8.6% a year

  • They see efficiency gains, lower funding costs and debt reduction supporting free cash flow, with room for shareholder returns once the balance sheet is in better shape.
  • The main watchpoints are currency swings in key markets, reliance on a small group of telecom customers, leverage, and the risk that new technologies or regulation could chip away at tower economics over time.

🐻 IHS Holding Bear Case

  • Supportive factors such as ongoing mobile infrastructure needs, cost control and diversification into other regions are recognised, but are not seen as enough to offset the risk that the agreed MTN cash offer effectively caps upside.
